walkthrough.title: Meet the Tiles
walkthrough.text: Welcome to A Gentle Rain. This is one of the most peaceful games you'll ever play. There's no competition, no stress — just the quiet satisfaction of watching flowers bloom on lily pads. Let's get set up. First, find the stack of 28 lake tiles. They have two sides. The front side shows a beautiful lily pad with four colored half-flowers along the edges. The back side is a calm green with subtle raindrop ripples. Go ahead and pick one up and look at both sides.
walkthrough.image_caption: Lake tiles — front side with lily pad flowers and green raindrop back

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now shuffle all 28 tiles face-down, green side up, so you cannot see the flower patterns. You can spread them out on the table and mix them around, then gather them into a neat stack. This is your draw pile for the whole game.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Blossom Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the 8 wooden blossom tokens. These are beautiful little discs, each showing a different colorful flower. Lay them all out face-up near the draw pile where everyone can see and reach them. Each one is unique — take a moment to admire them. These are the blossoms you are trying to coax into blooming during the game.

walkthrough.title: Place Your First Tile
walkthrough.text: Draw one tile from the top of the stack and flip it face-up. Place it in the center of the table. This is your starting tile and the beginning of your lake. Every tile you place from now on will connect to this one, growing outward like ripples on water.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: That is the entire setup. If you are playing solo, you will draw and place tiles one at a time. If you are playing with a friend, decide who goes first — you will take turns drawing and placing tiles together, cooperatively. The goal is simple and beautiful: place tiles so that matching colors meet at intersections, causing flowers to bloom. Let's learn how.

summary.title: Meet the Tiles
bullets: 28 lake tiles — front shows lily pad with 4 colored half-flower edges
Back is green with raindrop ripples

summary.title: Shuffle the Tiles
bullets: Shuffle all 28 tiles face-down (green side up)
Stack them into a draw pile

summary.title: Set Out the Blossom Tokens
bullets: Place all 8 wooden blossom tokens face-up nearby
Each token shows a unique flower

summary.title: Place Your First Tile
bullets: Draw 1 tile, flip face-up, place in center of table
This is the starting tile for your lake

summary.title: Ready to Play
bullets: Solo: draw and place tiles one at a time
2-player: alternate turns, playing cooperatively
Goal: match colors at intersections to bloom flowers

walkthrough.title: Draw a Tile
walkthrough.text: On your turn, draw one tile from the top of the face-down stack. Flip it over so you can see the lily pad side with its four colored half-flower edges. Take a moment to look at the colors and think about where it could go.

walkthrough.title: Place the Tile
walkthrough.text: Now place your tile next to any tile already on the table. It must touch at least one existing tile along an edge. Here is the key rule: wherever two tiles touch, the colored half-flowers on their touching edges must match. You can rotate your tile freely before placing it — turn it around to find the best fit. Tiles do not need to match on every side, only on the sides that actually touch another tile.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tiles showing colored half-flower edges that must match when adjacent

walkthrough.title: Mandatory Placement
walkthrough.text: If you can legally place your tile anywhere in the lake, you must place it. You cannot choose to discard a tile that has a valid spot. Take your time and look carefully — there may be a placement you did not notice at first.

walkthrough.title: Discarding a Tile
walkthrough.text: Sometimes you will draw a tile that simply cannot fit anywhere legally. Every touching edge must match colors, and if there is no spot where that works, you must discard the tile to a face-up discard pile. This is not a failure — it is just the gentle rhythm of the rain. Draw again on your next turn.

walkthrough.title: Blooming a Flower
walkthrough.text: This is the magical moment. When you complete a two-by-two square of four tiles, look at the center intersection where all four tile corners meet. Each corner has a quarter-circle cutout, and together they form a small circular space. If the four half-flowers surrounding that intersection create a complete color match, a blossom blooms. Choose the matching blossom token from the supply and place it in that intersection. It is a lovely little moment every time it happens.
walkthrough.image_caption: Blossom tokens placed when four tile corners complete a color match

walkthrough.title: One Bloom per Intersection
walkthrough.text: Each intersection can only hold one blossom token. If the matching blossom token has already been placed at a different intersection earlier in the game, then no bloom happens at this new intersection. That is okay — there are 8 unique tokens and many intersections to create.

walkthrough.title: Game End
walkthrough.text: The game ends in one of two ways. Either you place all 8 blossom tokens — congratulations, that is a perfect game and a beautiful achievement — or you run out of tiles in the draw pile. When the last tile is drawn and placed or discarded, the game is over.

walkthrough.title: Scoring
walkthrough.text: Your final score is the number of blossom tokens you placed plus the number of tiles remaining in the draw pile. In a perfect game where all 8 blossoms bloom, you also score points for every tile you did not need. The highest possible score comes from blooming all 8 flowers as quickly as possible, leaving the most tiles in the stack. But honestly, just blooming flowers is its own reward.

summary.title: Draw a Tile
bullets: Draw 1 tile from the face-down stack
Flip it face-up to see the lily pad side

summary.title: Place the Tile
bullets: Place adjacent to any existing tile (at least 1 edge touching)
All touching edges must match colors
Rotate freely before placing

summary.title: Mandatory Placement
bullets: If a legal placement exists, you must place the tile
You cannot voluntarily discard a playable tile

summary.title: Discarding a Tile
bullets: If no legal placement exists, discard the tile face-up
Discarded tiles are out of the game

summary.title: Blooming a Flower
bullets: Complete a 2×2 square of tiles
If colors match at the center intersection, a blossom blooms
Place the matching wooden blossom token in the intersection

summary.title: One Bloom per Intersection
bullets: Each intersection holds at most 1 blossom token
If the matching token is already placed elsewhere, no bloom occurs

summary.title: Game End
bullets: All 8 blossom tokens placed = perfect game
OR all 28 tiles drawn = game over

summary.title: Scoring
bullets: Score = blossom tokens placed + tiles remaining in stack
Perfect game: 8 blossoms + leftover tiles
Higher score = bloomed faster with more tiles to spare

walkthrough.title: Welcome — Your First Few Tiles
walkthrough.text: Take a breath. Welcome to A Gentle Rain. In this tutorial, we'll walk through a 2-player game, placing the first several tiles together so you can see how placement, rotation, and blooming work. If you're playing solo, just take both roles yourself — the rhythm is the same. There's no rush. There's no competition. Just the quiet joy of watching flowers bloom on a lake of lily pads.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Tile
walkthrough.text: Your lake already has one tile in the center from setup. Look at it. See the four colored half-flowers along its edges — one on each side. Each edge shows a different color. These colors are everything. When matching colors meet at an intersection of four tiles, a flower blooms. That's the whole game. Let's draw our first tile.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Drawing Your First Tile
walkthrough.text: Player 1, draw a tile from the top of the face-down stack and flip it over. Look at the tile in your hand. See those lily pads on the edges? Each one has a color. Your job is simple — place the tile so matching colors meet. But before you place it, try rotating it. Turn it 90 degrees. Then again. And again. Four orientations, four different ways the colors line up with the starting tile. Find the rotation that creates the most color matches on touching edges.
walkthrough.image_caption: A tile showing four colored half-flower edges

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Placing with Purpose
walkthrough.text: Player 1 rotates the tile carefully and places it to the right of the starting tile, matching the touching edge colors perfectly. One edge touches, one color matches. Good. But notice something — Player 1 didn't just match the touching edge. They also positioned the tile so the top and bottom edges create promising setups for future tiles. That's thinking ahead.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: The Quick Drop
walkthrough.text: Player 2 draws a tile, glances at it, and places it below the starting tile without rotating it. The touching edge happens to match — lucky. But look more closely at the other edges. If Player 2 had rotated the tile 90 degrees clockwise, it would have matched on the touching edge AND set up a color that lines up with Player 1's tile above. A bloom opportunity, missed.
walkthrough.image_caption: Two tiles placed — one edge matching, but a better rotation existed

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Rotate Before Placing
walkthrough.text: Before placing, look at all the edges touching your tile. Rotate the tile to see if a different orientation creates a color match — or better yet, sets up a match at a future intersection. One rotation could be the difference between a bloom and a missed opportunity. There's no timer. Take your time. Turn the tile in your hands. Feel the weight of the decision. Then place it gently.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Building the Grid
walkthrough.text: Player 1 draws again and places a tile above the starting tile, matching the touching edge. Now there are three tiles forming an L-shape — the starting tile in the center, one to the right, one above. Player 1 is building compactly, keeping the tiles close together. This creates intersections where four tiles can eventually meet.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Boxing In a Corner
walkthrough.text: Player 2 draws and places a tile extending the bottom edge further down and to the left, creating a long arm away from the cluster. It matches the touching edge, so it's legal. But now there's a tile sticking out with no neighbors on three sides. Future tiles placed out here won't connect to the main grid easily. Player 2 has boxed themselves into a corner — limiting where future tiles can usefully go.
walkthrough.image_caption: A tile placed far from the cluster, limiting future connections

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Keep Your Grid Open
walkthrough.text: Keep your grid open. Try to maintain edges where future tiles can connect to multiple neighbors at once. Boxing yourself into a corner — or stretching the lake into a long line — limits your options for the rest of the game. Build compactly. A tight grid means more intersections, and more intersections means more chances for flowers to bloom.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Setting Up a Bloom
walkthrough.text: Player 1 draws a tile and studies the lake carefully. Three tiles form an L-shape around an empty space. If Player 1 can fill that space, it will complete a two-by-two square. Player 1 rotates the new tile slowly — once, twice — and finds an orientation where the colors on all touching edges match. They place the tile. Four tiles now form a perfect square.

walkthrough.title: Checking for a Bloom
walkthrough.text: Look at the center of the two-by-two square. Four tile corners meet at one point. Each corner has a quarter-circle cutout with a colored half-flower. Do all four colors match? If they do, a flower blooms! Scan the blossom tokens in the supply, find the matching one, and place it gently in that intersection. Watch it nestle into the space between the four tiles. Beautiful.
walkthrough.image_caption: A blossom token placed at the center of a completed 2×2 square

walkthrough.title: Player 2: The Redemption
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has been watching and learning. This time, they draw a tile and pause. They look at the lake. They spot a spot next to the existing square where placing a tile would start forming a second two-by-two square. They rotate the tile carefully — checking each orientation against the touching edges. One rotation lines up perfectly. Not only do the touching edges match, but the corner colors align with the tiles already there. Player 2 places the tile. The setup is perfect for the next draw to complete another bloom.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player 2 carefully places a tile setting up a future bloom

walkthrough.title: A Flower Blooms for Player 2
walkthrough.text: Player 1 draws next and completes the second two-by-two square that Player 2 set up. The four corners meet — and the colors match! A second blossom token goes down. Player 2's careful rotation and thoughtful placement made this bloom happen. That's the magic of this game — patience and attention create beauty.

walkthrough.title: The Rhythm of the Game
walkthrough.text: You've now seen the full rhythm of A Gentle Rain. Draw a tile. Study it. Rotate it. Look at the lake. Find the placement that matches edges and builds toward future blooms. Place it gently. Check for blooms. Pass to your partner. Repeat. The game flows like a quiet conversation between you and the tiles.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations — you've placed your first tiles, watched Player 2 learn from two common missteps, and seen flowers bloom on your lake. Remember: always rotate before placing, keep your grid compact and open, and think about setting up future blooms, not just the current turn. You're ready to play a full game on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are two things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for placement advice and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Enjoy the rain.

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → placement advice for beginners
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Always Rotate Before Placing
walkthrough.text: This is the single most important habit in A Gentle Rain. Every tile has four edges with different colors. Before you commit to a placement, rotate the tile through all four orientations. Check each one against the touching edges. You are looking for the rotation that creates the most color matches — and ideally, the one that also sets up future bloom opportunities at nearby intersections. There is no timer. Take your time.

walkthrough.title: Keep the Grid Open
walkthrough.text: New players often build their lake in a long line or stretch it out in one direction. This severely limits blooming chances because flowers only bloom in two-by-two squares. Instead, build compactly. Keep tiles clustered together so you create as many potential intersections as possible. A tight, roughly rectangular shape gives you the most blooming opportunities with every new tile.

walkthrough.title: Focus on Bloom Opportunities
walkthrough.text: Don't just place tiles to place them. With every tile, ask yourself: does this placement bring me closer to completing a two-by-two square where the colors might match? Sometimes a legal placement technically works but contributes nothing toward a bloom. If you have a choice between a dead-end placement and one that builds toward an intersection, always choose the one that builds toward a bloom.

walkthrough.title: Track Your Remaining Flowers
walkthrough.text: There are 8 unique blossom tokens — one for each flower color. Once a token is placed, there is no benefit to completing another intersection of that same color. Keep an eye on which tokens are still in the supply. Focus your placements on creating intersections that match the colors of flowers you still need. In a perfect game, all 8 bloom.

walkthrough.title: Communicate in 2-Player Games
walkthrough.text: In a 2-player cooperative game, talk to your partner. Point out which intersections are almost complete, which colors you are building toward, and where you see promising spots forming. Since there is no hidden information, you can plan together completely openly. Two brains spotting opportunities is better than one.

walkthrough.title: Don't Stress About Discards
walkthrough.text: Sometimes you draw a tile that simply cannot fit anywhere legally. That is okay — it is part of the game's gentle rhythm. Discard it and move on. Do not force a bad placement just to avoid discarding. A poorly placed tile can block future bloom opportunities, which is worse than losing one tile to the discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Common Suboptimal Plays
walkthrough.text: Watch out for these three common mistakes. First, rushing placement without checking all rotations — you almost certainly missed a better orientation. Second, ignoring the grid shape and building in a line, which limits intersections. Third, closing off edges early by placing tiles that leave no room for future neighbors. All three reduce your bloom chances. Slow down, look at the whole lake, and think one tile ahead.

walkthrough.title: Color Frequency Analysis
walkthrough.text: Not all colors appear equally across the 28 tiles. Before you start, take a moment to understand the color distribution. Some colors appear on more tile edges than others, making them easier to match at intersections. Knowing which colors are abundant helps you prioritize — build toward intersections using common colors early, and save rare-color blooms for when you have more tiles placed and better information about what remains in the stack.

walkthrough.title: Grid Shape Optimization
walkthrough.text: The ideal lake shape for maximum blooming is a compact rectangle. A 4-by-7 or 5-by-6 rectangle uses all 28 tiles with the maximum number of internal intersections — each one a potential bloom. In practice, you won't achieve a perfect rectangle because tile colors dictate placement. But aim for it. Every tile that sticks out from the main body is a wasted intersection. Track your grid dimensions as you play and gently steer toward rectangular growth.

walkthrough.title: Tile Counting
walkthrough.text: As tiles are placed, you gain information about what remains in the stack. There are 28 tiles total. If you've placed 10 and discarded 2, there are 16 left. More importantly, pay attention to which colors you have seen. If you have already placed many tiles with blue edges and few with yellow, the remaining tiles likely have more yellow. This helps you predict what colors future tiles will offer and plan your intersections accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing Bloom Potential
walkthrough.text: The strongest placements are tiles that contribute to multiple partial intersections simultaneously. When you place a tile at the corner of your grid, it might only touch one intersection. But a tile placed in the interior of the grid can contribute to up to four different intersections at once. Prioritize interior placements whenever possible. Each tile placed in the middle of the grid does more work than one placed at the edge.

walkthrough.title: Perfect Game Strategy
walkthrough.text: A perfect game requires all 8 blossom tokens to be placed, ideally as quickly as possible to maximize leftover tiles in the score. This demands disciplined grid-building from tile one. Start compact, build rectangular, and aggressively pursue every bloom opportunity. Don't save up for a big payoff later — bloom early and often. Each early bloom reduces pressure on the remaining tiles. The fastest perfect games achieve all 8 blooms with 8-10 tiles still in the stack, scoring 16-18 points.

walkthrough.title: 2-Player Coordination: Dividing Color Responsibility
walkthrough.text: In a cooperative 2-player game, try assigning color zones. One player focuses on building intersections for certain flower colors while the other focuses on the remaining colors. This prevents both players from competing for the same intersections and ensures coverage across all 8 blossom types. Communicate which colors you are giving up on and which you are actively pursuing. Adjust assignments as tiles reveal themselves.

walkthrough.title: When to Sacrifice a Placement
walkthrough.text: Sometimes you draw a tile that can legally go in a spot, but placing it there would close off a high-value intersection you're building toward. In this case, it might be better to place the tile in a less optimal but still legal position — or even accept a suboptimal edge match — to keep your best intersections open for a future tile that fits better. Think of it as a short-term sacrifice for a long-term bloom. The tile you don't have yet might be the one that completes two blooms at once.

bullets: Not all colors appear equally across the 28 tiles
Common colors are easier to match — build those intersections first
Save rare-color blooms for when you have more information

Every tile sticking out is a wasted intersection
Steer toward rectangular growth as you play

summary.title: Maximizing Bloom Potential

entries.definition: One of 28 tiles in the game. Each tile is nearly square with quarter-circle cutouts at the corners. The front shows a lily pad with four colored half-flower edges. The back is green with subtle raindrop ripple patterns. Tiles are drawn one at a time from a face-down stack.
The face-down side of each lake tile, showing a calm green color with circular raindrop ripple patterns. Tiles are shuffled and stacked green-side-up to form the draw pile.

One of 8 unique wooden flower discs. Each shows a different colorful flower design. Blossom tokens are placed at tile intersections when a color match is completed. Collecting all 8 is a perfect game.

Each tile has four edges, each displaying a colored half-flower. When two tiles are placed adjacent, their touching edges must show the same color. The colors correspond to different flower types visible on the lily pad artwork.

A tile must be placed so that at least one edge touches an existing tile. All touching edges must match colors. The tile may be rotated freely before being placed. If a legal placement exists, it is mandatory.

Before placing a tile, you may rotate it to any orientation. Since each edge has a different color, rotation changes which colors face each direction. This is the primary strategic decision each turn.

When a drawn tile cannot be legally placed anywhere in the lake (no spot where all touching edges match), it must be discarded face-up. Discarded tiles are out of the game and count as played for scoring purposes.

When a 2×2 square of four tiles is completed and the colors at the center intersection match, a blossom blooms. The matching wooden blossom token is placed at that intersection. This is the core scoring mechanism of the game.

The point where four tile corners meet in a 2×2 square. Each tile has quarter-circle cutouts at its corners, creating a small circular space at the intersection. If the surrounding half-flowers match in color, a blossom token is placed here.

Achieved when all 8 blossom tokens are placed before the tile stack runs out. A perfect game means every unique flower has bloomed somewhere in your lake. The score for a perfect game is 8 plus the number of tiles remaining in the stack.
Calculated as: number of blossom tokens placed + number of unplayed tiles remaining in the draw stack. A higher score means you bloomed more flowers and did so efficiently, with tiles to spare.
The default way to play A Gentle Rain. One player draws and places all tiles, trying to bloom as many flowers as possible. The meditative pace makes it a perfect quiet activity.

Two players take turns drawing and placing tiles from the shared stack. Both players work together toward the same goal of blooming flowers. Communication and coordination are encouraged. The score is shared.
The face-down stack of shuffled lake tiles. Players draw one tile at a time from the top. The game ends when the draw pile is exhausted or all 8 blossoms bloom.
